coredata.dc:title: Multifunctional Eu-doped carbon dots nanoprobe for highly sensitive and selective determination of glutathione in biological fluid and cell imaging

coredata.dc:description: Glutathione (GSH) holds a vital role in biological systems, serving diverse cellular functions. Selective determination of GSH over cysteine (Cys) and homocysteine (Hcy) stays challenging due to the shared functional groups among these biothiols. In this study, a versatile Eu-doped carbon dots (Eu-CDs) nanoprobe has been developed for the exceptionally sensitive and selective detection of GSH in biological fluids. The synthesized Eu-CDs exhibited high solubility in water, stability to salt and pH variations, and consistent fluorescence emission, boasting a quantum yield of 40.67 %. Following the optimization of detection parameters, a robust linear relationship for GSH was established in the concentration range of 0.0–50 μM, with a detection limit of 0.03 μM. The viability of the technique in actual samples was evaluated by successfully measuring the GSH in urine and human serum samples with a range recovery from 90.6 % to 98.5 %. Furthermore, Eu-CDs proved to be exceptionally suitable for imaging Hela cells owing to their low cytotoxicity and remarkable biocompatibility. As a result, the developed fluorescent biosensor demonstrated significant potential for widespread application in clinical diagnostic analyses.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Glutathione (GSH) holds a vital role in biological systems, serving diverse cellular functions. Selective determination of GSH over cysteine (Cys) and homocysteine (Hcy) stays challenging due to the shared functional groups among these biothiols. In this study, a versatile Eu-doped carbon dots (Eu-CDs) nanoprobe has been developed for the exceptionally sensitive and selective detection of GSH in biological fluids. The synthesized Eu-CDs exhibited high solubility in water, stability to salt and pH variations, and consistent fluorescence emission, boasting a quantum yield of 40.67 %. Following the optimization of detection parameters, a robust linear relationship for GSH was established in the concentration range of 0.0–50 μM, with a detection limit of 0.03 μM. The viability of the technique in actual samples was evaluated by successfully measuring the GSH in urine and human serum samples with a range recovery from 90.6 % to 98.5 %. Furthermore, Eu-CDs proved to be exceptionally suitable for imaging Hela cells owing to their low cytotoxicity and remarkable biocompatibility. As a result, the developed fluorescent biosensor demonstrated significant potential for widespread application in clinical diagnostic analyses.

ce:section.ce:para: Amino acids, as small biomolecules, hold significance in human physiological processes, with their abnormal expression often closely linked to various diseases [1]. One noteworthy example is Glutathione (GSH), a thiol-containing polypeptide crucial for regulating cellular metabolism. The distinctive active sulfhydryl (−SH) functional group within GSH plays a pivotal role in safeguarding essential enzyme proteins from oxidation, preventing their inactivation; this protection ensures the maintenance of energy metabolism and cellular utilization [2,3]. The diminution in intracellular GSH levels functions as a precursory signal for the initiation of apoptosis [4]. Besides, specific malignant tumor cells manifest an elevated abundance of GSH in comparison to their normal counterparts, establishing GSH as a pertinent biomolecular marker for the detection and evaluation of cancer [5,6]. Of equal importance, alterations in GSH levels are intricately associated with a spectrum of diseases, encompassing leucocyte depletion, diabetes, Parkinson's, and Alzheimer's disease [7,8]. So, it is imperative to investigate simple, precise, and efficient methods for detecting GSH in biological samples, ensuring accurate results, an extensive linear range and a minimal detection limit; this undertaking is fundamental for advancing disease surveillance and prevention efforts [9]. To day, various alternative approaches have been recognised for the detection of GSH, inclusive of high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [10], electrochemistry [11], surface-enhanced Raman scattering spectroscopy [12], colorimetry [13,14], and fluorescence spectrometry [15]. Fluorescence spectrometry stands out among these methods due to its distinct benefits, including heightened sensitivity, ease of use, swiftness, and cost-effectiveness in detection [16]. Nearly all fluorometric approaches can identify GSH through the utilization of diverse fluorescent sensors, including hybrid fluorescent dyad [17], silver/gold nanoclusters [18], near-infrared probes [19], and covalent organic frameworks (COFs) [20]. Nevertheless, the sensors mentioned above still present several drawbacks. These include the need for complex modification in a hybrid fluorescent dyad, increased expense for gold/silver nano-clusters, and an elaborate and time-intensive synthetic pathway for COFs and near-infrared nanosensors. Therefore, there is a strong demand for the development of a novel GSH detection probe that utilizes simple, cost-effective, and rapidly responsive methods.

Carbon dots (CDs) have garnered extensive attention due to their appealing optical characteristics, compact size, affordability, favorable biocompatibility, and straightforward synthesis from a variety of natural sources [21]. The ability to tune their color in photoluminescence (PL), coupled with outstanding photostability, non-toxicity, and convenient functionalization, positions CDs as promising options for diverse applications, including bioimaging, biomedicine, photocatalysis, displays, and optoelectronics [22,23]. The optical properties of CDs may differ readily altered by the choice of precursors and synthesis methods, providing CDs with the benefit of easy surface modifications. Over the last decade, significant endeavors have been undertaken to enhance the photoluminescent properties and investigate potential application domains for CD materials [24,25]. Moreover, owing to the exceptional physical and chemical attributes of CDs, they have emerged as a novel fluorescent nanomaterial with the potential to substitute conventional organic dyes, hazardous semiconductor quantum dots, and less potent fluorescent polymers [26,27]. Being a significant lanthanide ion, Eu(III) exhibits a specific affinity for oxygen-donor atoms, allowing it to coordinate with carboxylic groups [28].

Nevertheless, the majority of methods based on carbon nanomaterials encounter drawbacks, including intricate synthesis, laborious post-surface modification processes, and reliance on a single testing mode. For example, GQDs of varying sizes have been identified as effective ratiometric fluorescence sensors. The synthesis of Eu-GQDs from GQDs involves three post-functionalization steps, leading to a complex synthesis process, potential side reactions, and reduced productivity [29]. However, owing to the limitations in the design-ability concept of extended wavelength fluorescent CDs, the emitted light of the majority of described CDs is spread across the blue and green regions. Consequently, the extensive utilization of these CDs in the imaging of biology and the detection of biological models face obstacles due to the inherent fluorescence emanating from proteins, amino acids, and other compounds found in tissue or biological specimens [30]. Therefore, there is a continued need to explore and develop a straightforward hydrothermal synthesis method for generating fluorescent carbon dots that emit longer-wavelength light in the yellow-red spectrum.

In this study, we have devised a straightforward one-step method for synthesizing Eu-CDs and determining GSH through the fluorescence (FL) method. Eu-CDs were synthesized using o-phenylenediamine (o-PD) and europium nitrate hexahydrate (Scheme 1 a). Notably, our synthesis route does not necessitate elevated high temperatures or extended durations compared to other work [24,28], offering advantages for the development of biosensing systems. The resulting water-soluble Eu-CDs exhibited yellow FL at 560 nm when excited at 430 nm. Furthermore, these Eu-CDs demonstrated outstanding stability under varying salt concentrations and temperatures, with a quantum yield (QY) of 40.67 %. Following an introduction of GSH, the FL indicator of Eu-CDs was quenched (Scheme 1b), validating the sensitivity and specificity of our method for monitoring GSH. Significantly, the presented method proved effective in accurately analyzing GSH in biological samples. Additionally, Eu-CDs were employed, showcasing their potential utility in hela cell imaging.

All of the compounds were utilized without undergoing additional purification procedures; all of them were of analytical grade. O-Phenylenediamine (o-PD), glutathione, valine (Val), proline (Pro), alanine (Ala), cystine (Cys), arginine (Arg), aspartic Acid (Asp), methionine (Met), threonine (Thre), amylase (Amy), serine (Ser), leucine (Leu), homocysteine (Hcy), and phenylalanine (Phe) were supplied from Shanghai Maclean Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. Urease (Ure) was bought from Shanghai Xinyu Biological Technology Co., Ltd. Trypsin (Try) was provided from Mreda Technology Inc. Glucose (Gluc), histidine (His), malic acid (Mal), and glutamic acid (Glu) were obtained from Sino Pharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. CuSO4.5H2O, sodium chloride, Co (NO3)2.6H2O, and Eu (NO3)3.6H2O were sourced from Lian Long (Tianjin) Industrial Co., Ltd. Phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) was used as buffer for all experiments. All of the test tubes were prepared by employing deionized water.

UV–visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectrum was made using a UV-3600 Plus UV–Vis–NIR spectrophotometer. Fluorescence was conducted employing a comprehensive Fluoro Full-featured steady-state spectrometer of rapid fluorescence featuring a 1 cm quartz prison cell and a slit size of 5/5 nm. The morphology of the Eu-CDs was investigated via transmission electron microscopy (TEM) utilizing a TecnaiG2 TF20 device. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was achieved with an ESCALAB 250Xi X-ray photoelectron spectrometer prepared through an Al Ka stimulating source. A Nexus 870 spectrometer with KBr pellets was utilized to document the Fourier transform infrared spectra (FTIR). Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were produced by utilizing CuKα (=0.154056) radioactivity. Raman spectroscopy was achieved by utilizing model IFS120HR, Bruker, Germany. Fluorescence lifetime analysis was carried out with the FL S590 spectrometers time-correlated single-photon-counting (TCSPC) apparatus from Edinburg Tests Ltd, U.K. The zeta potential measurement of the Eu-CDs was determined utilizing a Zeta Sizer Nano ZS device (Malvern, UK). Cell imaging had been verified via the Olympus Fluoview (FV 1200, Olympus Co. Ltd., Japan).

By utilizing hydrothermal treatment and temperature, the Eu-CDs were synthesized by dissolving 300 mg of o-PD and 75 mg of europium nitrate hexahydrate in 30 mL deionized water. Following a 5 min sonication, the mixture was subsequently relocated to a poly(tetrafluoroethylene) (Teflon)-lined autoclave and exposed to 160 °C for a duration of 4 h. After lowering the autoclave temperature to room level, the solution containing Eu-CDs underwent centrifugation at 10,000 rpm for 15 min to eliminate impurities. The material obtained was filtered utilizing a 0.22 μm strainer membrane and dialyzed using a 1000 Da dialysis membrane for a period of 6 h. To facilitate additional characterization and various applications, the Eu-CDs were kept in a refrigerator at 4 °C. The CDs were prepared by the same method mentioned above without europium nitrate hexahydrate. For subsequent experiments, a segment of the purified Eu-CDs solution was subjected to freeze-drying, followed by crushing and pulverization for FT-IR, XPS, and XRD analysis.

The process entails putting a liquid solution in a region of integration, where a xenon lamp generates light dispersed into distinct wavelengths. This light illuminates the sample. Through an optical fiber. Upon sample excitation, fluorescence is emitted and captured by a spectral detecting mechanism. The instrument's software calculates quantum yield by comparing emitted to absorbed photons, determining fluorescence efficiency. Fundamentally, the determination of fluorescence quantum yield involves dividing the number of emitted photons by the number of absorbed photons.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of GSH based on Eu-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The detailed procedures for the detection of GSH by the Eu-CDs were in the following manner: First, 10 μL of the Eu-CDs diluted with deionized water was added to 1.0 mL of 20 mM PBS (pH = 7.4). Secondly, a mixture of 3 mM different concentrations of GSH that was pre-incubated for 2 min was introduced. Immediately afterward, the fluorescence measures were performed at an excitation/emission wavelength of 430/560 nm with a slit width of 5/5 nm. For the selective experiment of GSH, the detection procedures were similar to the sensitivity mentioned above assay.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of GSH in biological fluid
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the viability of this approach for analyzing GSH in human serum and urine, specific sample preparations were undertaken. Human serum and urine samples were obtained from a healthy volunteer at Gansu Provincial Hospital in Lanzhou, China. The removal of large particles from both human serum and urine samples involved concentrating at 8000 rpm for 10 min, followed by filtering via a 0.22 μm strainer membrane. Subsequently, the samples were diluted in PBS solution (20 mM, pH 7.4) at a 20-time dilution before monitoring. 10 μL volume of Eu-CDs was added, and the solution was able to stabilize at ambient temperature for 2 min. The fluorescence signals were then measured at 430 nm excitation and 560 nm emission. The precision of the technique for GSH measurement in human serum and urine was validated using the standard addition method.

The MTT standard examination was conducted to evaluate the viability of the Eu-CDs solution on Hela cells. Initially, Hela cells were taken in the logarithmic growth phase, and the cell suspension concentration was adjusted to approximately 5 × 104 cells mL−1. Subsequently, 200 μl of the cell suspension was introduced to each well of a 96-well culture plate. The plates were then placed in a 5 % CO2 environment and hatched at 37 °C for approximately 24 h until the cells formed a monolayer. Following the monolayer formation, a range of concentrations (10, 20, 50, 100, 200, 400, 800, 1000 μg ml−1) of Eu-CDs solution, obtained by diluting with PBS, was added to the wells. Six replicate wells were established for each concentration. After an additional 24h period of incubation, 20 μl of MTT solution (5 mg mL−1) was introduced to each well and put in a 5 % CO2, 37 °C test tube for 4 h. The culture was then terminated, and the supernatant was discarded. Subsequently, 200 μl of DMSO was incorporated into each well, and the plates were positioned on a shaker, oscillating at low speed for 10 min to ensure complete dissolution of the crystals. The absorbance (OD) value of each well was quantified at 492 nm via a microplate scanner. Additionally, a standard cell control was included in the experiment.

The manufacture of Eu-CDs was characterized using UV–vis spectrophotometry, fluorescence spectroscopy, FT-IR, XPS, TEM, XRD, and Raman spectroscopy. As illustrated in Fig. S1a, the optical properties determined by UV–vis absorbance of Eu-CDs manifest the presence of dual absorption peaks at 231 and 290 nm. These peaks can be ascribed to the π − π* transition of the aromatic sp2 domains, encompassing the CC and CN bonds and the n- π* transition of the CO bond, respectively [31]. When the fluorescence excitation peak was set to 430 nm, Eu-CDs showed a fluorescence emission peak at 560 nm and displayed a yellow color when exposed to a handheld UV lamp. as can be seen in Fig. S1b. As depicted in Fig. 1 a, the examination of fluorescence strength exhibited by Eu-CDs under different excitation wavelengths ranging from 380 to 500 nm. The findings obtainable in the figure reveal a consistent fluorescence intensity, regardless of the excitation wavelength. This remark implies the absence of emissions linked to the precise excitation wavelengths employed in this investigation. In the 2D-emission plots, it is evident that a photoluminescence center is detectable at the emission wavelength of 560 nm and excitation wavelength of 430 nm, as illustrated in (Fig. 1b). The CIE color coordinates of Eu-CDs are (0.433 0.534), providing additional confirmation that Eu-CDs exhibit yellow fluorescence under UV radiation (Fig. 1c). The analysis of the structural composition and functional moieties of the created material was conducted using FT-IR spectrophotometry. As depicted in Fig. 1d, the assignment of these groups can be attributed to the stretching and bending vibration modes, specifically N–H/O–H (3405 cm−1), CH2 (2925 cm−1), CO/CN (1622 cm−1,1591 cm−1), C–C (1503 cm−1), C–N (1356 cm−1), C–O (1273 cm−1), and C–H (750 cm−1) [24,32,33]. The outcomes of the FTIR analysis unequivocally validate the heightened solubility of Eu-CDs in water, attributable to the prevalence of carboxyl, hydroxyl, and amino functional groups on their surface. Furthermore, the presence of nitrogen is instrumental in stabilizing the photic characteristics and the surface state of nanosensors, thereby facilitating the manifestation of outstanding luminescent properties [34].

The XPS investigation was utilized to investigate the surface chemical structure of the Eu-CDs composite, as depicted in Fig. 2 . The survey spectra of Eu-CDs, illustrated in Fig. 2a, reveal the presence of C, O, N, and Eu elements in the synthesized composite. As depicted in Fig. 2b, spectral analysis elucidated the elemental composition of Eu-CDs, indicating the presence of carbon, oxygen, nitrogen, and europium. The atomic percentages for these elements were determined to be 77.25 %, 13.7 %, 7.36 %, and 1.69 %, respectively. The high-resolution C1s XPS analysis, as depicted in Fig. 2c, exhibits three discernible peaks situated at 289.2 eV, 286.4 eV, and 284.7 eV. These peaks are ascribed to CO, C–N/C–O, CC/C–C, and functionalities, respectively. Meanwhile, the N1s XPS spectrum, delineated in Fig. 2d–is amenable to deconvolution into two distinct peaks situated at 398.6 eV and 401.02 eV, corresponding to amino N and graphite N, respectively [32,35–37]. Likewise, the O 1s peak (depicted in Fig. 2e) was examined, revealing two discernible peaks at 531.5 eV and 533.09 eV, indicative of CO and C–O bonds, respectively [38]. In Fig. 2f, the binding energies corresponding to Eu are attributed to Eu3+ 3d5/2 (1134.9 eV) and Eu3+ 3d3/2 (1164.5 eV) [39]. Consistently, the results from both FTIR and XPS analyses exhibited strong agreement.

In order to attain a more profound understanding of the structural and compositional attributes of Eu-CDs, XRD and Raman spectroscopy were employed for the analysis and characterization of surface functional groups. As depicted in Fig. S2a, the scrutiny of XRD patterns disclosed that the acquired materials manifest powder XRD patterns characterized by numerous distinct peaks. This observation provides compelling evidence attesting to their crystalline nature [40]. In Fig. S2b, the D band is linked to the vibrational modes of carbon atoms possessing dangling bonds within the terminal plane of disordered graphite. Conversely, the G band is correlated with the vibrational modes of sp2 carbon atoms within a two-dimensional (2D) hexagonal lattice [41].

The analysis of morphology and particle size was conducted through the application of TEM. Fig. 3 a and b illustrated well-shaped and evenly dispersed Eu-CDs. The HRTEM image showed lattice strips of carbon dots at 0.23 nm intervals, aligning precisely with the 100-crystal level of graphite (Fig. 3b inset). The analysis of the inverse fast Fourier transform (IFFT) in Fig. 3c substantiates the presence of lattice strips at a dimension of 0.23 nm, showcasing a regular spacing of 1.194 nm [42]. The size distribution histogram (Fig. 3d) shows Eu-CDs with sizes with diameters spanning from 0.5 to 7.6 nm and exhibiting an average size of 2.7 nm.

The stability assessment of the freshly synthesized Eu-CDs was conducted under diverse conditions to enhance our comprehension of their functionality. Fig. S3a illustrates that, even in the presence of a high NaCl concentration (100 mM) within the solution, the FL signal of Eu-CDs remained remarkably stable, affirming their outstanding salt tolerance. Furthermore, the impact of pH on FL intensity was systematically investigated. Evident from Fig. S3b, the fluorescence intensity exhibited a discernible increase within the pH range spanning from 4.0 to 12.0 using PBS buffer, likewise in Fig. S4 the signals of Eu-CDs at various pH values of britton robinson buffer displayed a discernible increase within the pH range spanning from 2.0 to 9.0 and decrease in range 10.0–12.0. These variations are likely attributed to the processes of protonation and deprotonation occurring at the terminal amino groups situated on the surface of the prepared material [43]. The Eu-CDs displayed sustained fluorescence intensity for 60 min under 430 nm light, as shown in Fig. S3c, indicating resistance to photobleaching and excellent stability during storage. The conclusive stability analysis involved examining the impact of temperature on the fluorescence intensity. As evident in Fig. S3d, the FL intensity remained nearly constant within the temperature range of 25–50 °C.

The fluorescence intensity of Eu-CDs experienced attenuation following the inclusion of various concentrations of GSH. Following the optimization of the response time and pH, the sensitivity of this phenomenon was explored. Additional investigations were conducted using two distinct GSH concentrations (20 and 90 μM), and various pH ranges were examined in a PBS buffer, spanning from 4.0 to 12.0 (Fig. 4 a and b). Consequently, a reaction time of 2 min and pH 7.4 were identified as the optimal conditions. Fluorescence intensities of Eu-CDs at 430 nm were monitored as GSH was introduced at concentrations ranging from 0 to 110 μM, and its impact on fluorescence intensity was scrutinized. Additionally, as depicted in Fig. 4c, there was a notable reduction in fluorescence intensity with an increase in GSH concentration. Within the concentration range of 0.0–50 μM, a robust linear relationship was observed between the quenching efficiencies (F0–F)/F0 and GSH concentrations, exhibiting a high linear correlation impact (R2 = 0.9959) (Fig. 4d). Here, F0 and F represent the fluorescence intensities of Eu-CDs at 430 nm before and after the addition of GSH, respectively. The limit of detection (LOD) was detected to be 0.03 μM, which is lower compared to findings in previous reports on GSH research studies.

Table 1 presents the sensor system employed in this investigation, utilizing Eu-CDs compared to previously reported methods. The system demonstrated notable advantages, such as rapid response times, a lower limit of detection with a wide range, and a cost-effective profile than previously published values. The Eu-CDs exhibit sensitivity to GSH through specific physical and chemical interactions. Physically, GSH modifies the photoluminescence of Eu-CDs by changing the electron density around Eu-CDs, which affects luminescence efficiency and quantum yield [2,44,45]. Chemically, the interaction between GSH and Eu-CDs involves the formation of complexes facilitated by GSH thiol. This interaction induces alterations in the local electronic environment, consequently impacting the fluorescence properties of the system. Likewise, the carboxyl group present on the surface of Eu-CDs engages in an electrostatic interaction with the positively charged nitrogen atoms of the GSH molecule. This interaction induces changes in charge distribution and energy states, ultimately leading to the quenching of fluorescence emitted by the Eu-CDs [46,47]. Furthermore, the Eu exhibit a pronounced affinity for bio thiols, indicating a preference for this thiophilic metal ion. Based on findings in the literature, it can be inferred that variations in the size of GSH and its nucleophilic capacity within the context of Eu-CDs interactions may contribute to the selective differentiation of GSH from Hcy and Cys [15,48]. These interactions, which may involve charge transfer mechanisms where the thiol group transfers charge to europium, make Eu-CDs effective for selective detection of GSH in biological systems. The straightforward usage and heightened sensitivity render this technique highly applicable for clinical diagnosis. Moreover, this methodology obviates the necessity for supplementary modifications to the detecting probe, thereby streamlining the detection procedure.

The specific identification of GSH in biological systems held significant importance. Nonetheless, the distinct determination of GSH in the attendance of Cys and Hcy remained a formidable challenge, given that all bio thiols share common thiol, carboxylic, and amino groups [31]. Moreover, the majority of methods for detecting biological thiols using carbon dots typically relied on heavy metal ions like Ag+ and Hg2+ [9,15]. However, the use of such ions holds promise risks to both the environment and human health. Consequently, there was a pressing need to create innovative and straightforward GSH sensing systems that exhibit excellent selectivity, addressing the specific demands within the realms of biology, clinical applications, and medicine. To assess the selectivity of Eu-CDs for GSH, interference assays were conducted under identical conditions using various natural amino acids, metal ions, and enzymes, including Gly, His, Try, Leu, Val, Arg, Asp, Phe, Ser, Gluc, Ala, Met, Thr, Cys, Glu, Lys, Hcy, Co, Na, Cu, Try, Ure, and Amy. As depicted in Fig. 5 a and b, the F0/F ratio indicated that the introduction of 270 μM amino acids, 100 μM metal ions, and 7 mg/L enzymes, the interferents did not result in significant changes in fluorescence. Furthermore, the fluorescence spectra of a mixture containing Eu-CDs, GSH, Cys, and Hcy were examined. The findings revealed that Hcy and Cys did not noticeably interfere with the selective performance for GSH (Fig. S5a). Even in the presence of competitive bio thiols like Cys, there was no apparent fluorescence decrease, underscoring the specificity of the carbon dots for GSH. Moreover, the selectivity of GSH based on CDs containing various amino acids, metal ions, and enzymes under identical conditions was investigated, as depicted in Fig. S5b. Some certain molecules have a notably influenced on the FL intensity and the FL attributed to GSH did not exhibited a full decrease as quencher when compared to its selectivity based on Eu-CDs. Overall, these findings distinctly establish the strong selectivity of Eu-CDs for GSH.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Principal study of GSH sensing strategy
ce:section.ce:para: In the realm of luminescent materials, the emitted FL can undergo quenching through four well-established mechanisms: Förster resonant energy transfer (FRET), the inner filter effect (IFE), dynamic quenching effect (DQE), and static quenching effect (SQE) [52]. SQE occurs when the fluorophore establishes a durable complex with another particle, resulting in a loss of fluorescence. On the other hand, DQE involves the fluorescence of material being lost and returning to the initial state owing to collisions between the quencher and the fluorescent molecule [53]. Suppose an overlap is detected among the excitation or emission spectra of the fluorescent material and the UV–vis absorption spectrum of the quencher. In that case, it suggests that the quenching may originate from FRET or the IFE [54]. Since there is no overlap among the absorption range of the quencher GSH and the fluorescence strength level of the Eu-CDs (Fig. 6 a), mechanisms like IFE or FRET can be ruled out.

To delve deeper into the fluorescence quenching mechanism, the data pertaining to fluorescence quenching were subjected to analysis using the Stern-Volmer (SV) plot. This choice was motivated by the fact that both static quenching (SQ) and dynamic quenching (DQ) can be theoretically elucidated through the Stern-Volmer equation (i.e., Eq. (1)) [54].
(1) ΔF = 1 + K
sv [Q]

Under both conditions, with and without the quencher, the FL signals are represented as ΔF, while [Q] denotes the quencher concentration. As depicted in Fig. 6b, the FL signals ratio ΔF exhibits a linear relationship (Y = 0.01485 GSH (μM) + 0.05663, R2 = 0.99167). This linearity observed implies that the fluorescence quenching of the Eu-CDs by GSH is attributable to either single dynamic or static quenching [52]. Additionally, an investigation into the FL lifetime of the Eu-CDs was conducted both in the absence and presence of GSH to clarify the specific type of FL quenching responsible for the observed effect on the Eu-CDs. In Fig. 6c and Table S1, it is evident that the average fluorescence lifetimes of the Eu-CDs before and after the introduction of GSH (90 μM) remained unchanged this observation suggests that the FL of Eu-CDs is statically quenched by GSH [55–57]. The zeta potential measurement, illustrated in Fig. 6d, indicates a change from 14.15 mV to −4.37 mV upon the addition of GSH.

Furthermore, As shown in Fig. S6a, the fluorescence of Eu-CDs diminishes with an escalating concentration of GSH. The absorption peak at approximately 287 nm, corresponding to Eu-CDs, vanishes upon the introduction of GSH Fig. S6b. Following a comprehensive scrutiny of the various fluorescent quenching mechanisms elucidated in this study, it is deduced that the predominant quenching mechanism exerted on Eu-CDs by GSH is attributable to SQE rather than DQE [58].

The analysis of GSH quantities in urine and human serum samples was conducted through the proposed methodology utilizing Eu-CDs. In optimal conditions, the findings outlined in Table 2 indicate that the initial concentrations of GSH in urine and human serum were 1.97 μM and 2.7 μM, respectively. Employing the standard addition technique, the average recoveries of GSH in these samples exhibited a range from 90.6 % to 98.5 %. Moreover, the relative standard deviation (RSD) consistently stayed below 2.45 %, a threshold deemed acceptable for quantitative analyses conducted on biological specimens in accordance with established academic standards. These results affirm the precision of our suggested GSH sensing technology, underscoring its considerable potential in academic research and applications.

The toxicity assay is crucial for evaluating the applicability of materials in both in vivo and in vitro settings. MTT assays were conducted to investigate the cytotoxic effects of Eu-CDs on Hela cells. As anticipated, the results indicated that Eu-CDs exhibited favorable biocompatibility. As demonstrated in Fig. S7, undergoing treatment with Eu-CDs for a duration of 24 h, even at a significant amount of 1000 μg mL−1, resulted that the existence of Eu-CDs did not exhibit a suppressive impact on the cells rather it promoted cell proliferation, aligning with the findings derived from assessments of cell viability and growth. As depicted in Fig. 7 , microscopic fluorescence images of only Hela cells (Fig. 7a), and HeLa cells pretreated with N-methylmaleimide (NMM; 1 mM) for 30 min (Fig. 7b). In Fig. 7c and d when HeLa cells pretreated with N-methylmaleimide (NMM; 1 mM) for 30 min and then incubated with Eu-CDs (1000 μg/mL) for 30 min and 2h, under typical circumstances (37 °C, 5 % CO2) the bright red fluorescence was observed. To provide stronger evidence for the internalization of Eu-CDs by cells, as illustrated in Fig. 8 , fluorescence microscopy images were taken of HeLa cells after pretreated with N-methylmaleimide (NMM; 1 mM) for 30 min then following incubation with Eu-CDs (1000 μg/mL) for varying durations (10 min, 20 min, 30 min, 1 h, 2 h, 4 h, 6 h, 8 h, 10 h, 12 h and 24 h) at 37 °C. The maximal internalization within HeLa cells occurred at 20 min post-incubation, indicating a rapid internalization of Eu-CDs. noteworthy fluorescence signals in the red-light channel were prominently observed within the intracellular regions. This observation unequivocally verifies the exceptional cell imaging capabilities of Eu-CDs, positioning them as a promising stage for facilitating diverse biological applications.

In summary, a straightforward, rapid, and uncomplicated fluorometric method for detecting GSH based on Eu-CDs was effectively developed. Using a one-step hydrothermal synthesis with o-PD and europium nitrate hexahydrate, fluorescent Eu-CDs were prepared and characterized by remarkable optical and chemical characteristics. The Eu-CDs produced yellow fluorescence with a wavelength of 560 nm when excited at 430 nm and displayed a consistent morphology and excellent dispersion capability in an aqueous solution. Following the implementation of GSH, there was a notable decrease in the fluorescence intensity of Eu-CDs. It was observed that other thiols (Cys and Hcy) did not exhibit any interference with GSH, thereby rendering it preferable for the utilization of Eu-CDs in GSH detection. This detection method encompasses a linear range from 0.0 to 50.0 μM, with a detection limit of 0.03 μM. Besides, this developed approach was successfully employed to detect GSH in human urine and serum samples, yielding acceptable results. The Eu-CDs demonstrate excellent biocompatibility and lack cytotoxicity, underscoring their importance in biomedical applications. Furthermore, with their superior sensing capabilities, we expect that this research will introduce a novel conceptual framework for designing uncomplicated nanostructures. Additionally, it offers a sustainable and environmentally friendly synthetic alternative for monitoring GSH and cell imaging.
